mnemonics.title: Kahn's Algorithm Steps
mnemonics.content: KQIS: Keep Queue of In-degree Zero (KQIZ)

mnemonics.title: Topological Sort Properties
mnemonics.content: DAG: Direction Acyclic Graph - if there's Direction, there's no Cycle

acronyms.usage: A directed graph with no cycles, used for modeling dependencies
Algorithm used for exploring graphs and finding topological order
Algorithm used in Kahn's topological sort approach
Alternative name for Kahn's topological sort algorithm

visualTips.visualization: Imagine dominoes falling in a line - each domino (vertex) can only fall after all dominoes pointing to it have fallen. The falling order is a topological sort.
Think of course prerequisites as a flowchart where arrows show which course must be completed before another. A valid semester plan is a topological ordering.
Source vertices (in-degree 0) are like tasks with no prerequisites - they have nothing to wait for and can start immediately.
Think of removing leaves from a tree - vertices with zero in-degree are leaves, remove them, update degrees, repeat until no vertices left.

analogies.analogy: Like following a recipe where ingredients must be prepared in correct order - you can't add eggs before beating them, can't bake before mixing batter.
Like building a house: foundation → walls → roof → painting. Each step depends on previous ones - this is a DAG structure.
Source vertices are like tasks that have no prerequisites in a project - they can start immediately without waiting for anything else.
A cycle in task dependencies is like promising: "I'll do this after you do that after I do this..." - impossible to start!
If you have two tasks with no dependency on each other (like doing laundry and cooking dinner), they can be done in either order - multiple topological sorts exist.
